sections.heading: Traditional methods became less common, mostly because withdrawal fell
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, use of traditional contraception went down from 9.8 percent in 2022 to 8.1 percent in 2025. Withdrawal made up most traditional use in both years, dropping from 8.1 percent to 6.8 percent. Calendar or rhythm was much less common, falling from 1.7 percent to 1.3 percent. Other traditional methods stayed at 0.0 percent in both years, so the national decline was mainly about withdrawal, with a smaller drop in calendar or rhythm.

sections.heading: Several regions had high traditional use, not just one
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was highest in Eastern Visayas at 12.1 percent. It was followed by Ilocos Region at 11.8 percent and Western Visayas at 10.8 percent. Central Luzon came next at 10.1 percent, while CALABARZON and Bicol Region were both at 9.7 percent. These higher-use regions are spread across Luzon and the Visayas, so high traditional use is not limited to one island group or one urban-rural profile. In the two highest regions, withdrawal made up most of the level, reaching 10.9 percent in Eastern Visayas and 10.8 percent in Ilocos Region.

sections.heading: Some regions were far below the national level
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest traditional contraceptive use at 3.2 percent, which was less than half the national level of 8.1 percent. Cagayan Valley at 4.1 percent, Central Visayas at 4.8 percent, NIR at 5.2 percent, MIMAROPA at 5.5 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 5.6 percent were also well below the national figure. These regions were not low because only one traditional method was missing; both withdrawal and calendar or rhythm were generally limited in the lower-use group. The gap between Eastern Visayas and BARMM was 8.9 percentage points, larger than the national traditional-use level itself.

sections.heading: Most regional changes were drops
sections.body: Most regions had lower traditional contraceptive use in 2025 than in 2022, but the size of the drop differed a lot. BARMM fell from 8.9 percent to 3.2 percent, Central Visayas from 10.2 percent to 4.8 percent, and Bicol Region from 14.3 percent to 9.7 percent. MIMAROPA also dropped from 8.7 percent to 5.5 percent. Only a few regions increased, and the largest rise was in Caraga, from 6.5 percent to 7.4 percent.

sections.heading: Traditional use was higher from the 30s through the early 40s
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was very low among women aged 15 to 19, at 0.6 percent, then rose to 4.5 percent among women aged 20 to 24. It reached 11.5 percent at ages 30 to 34 and 12.7 percent at both ages 35 to 39 and 40 to 44. It then eased to 10.0 percent at ages 45 to 49. Across these age groups, withdrawal was still the larger traditional method. Calendar or rhythm became more visible at older ages, reaching 3.0 percent at ages 45 to 49.

sections.heading: Use was highest among women with one or two children
sections.body: Looking at number of living children, traditional use was highest among women with one or two children, at 14.7 percent in 2025. It was lower among women with three or four children at 11.0 percent and among women with five or more children at 10.9 percent. Women with no living children had much lower traditional use at 1.8 percent, which reflects the all-women denominator as well as stage of family building. The sharpest declines from 2022 were among women with five or more children, down 4.4 percentage points, and women with three or four children, down 3.5 points.

sections.heading: Urban and rural levels were close compared with regional gaps
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use was 8.6 percent among urban women and 7.5 percent among rural women in 2025. Both were lower than in 2022, when urban women were at 10.2 percent and rural women were at 9.4 percent. Withdrawal again made up most of the level in both places, at 7.1 percent in urban areas and 6.3 percent in rural areas. The 1.1-point urban-rural gap was small compared with the much wider differences across regions.

sections.heading: Traditional use did not rise neatly with education
sections.body: Traditional use was highest among women with post-secondary education, at 13.9 percent in 2025. College graduates were lower at 9.0 percent. Women with Grades 7 to 10 were at 8.5 percent, Grades 1 to 6 at 7.8 percent, and no education at 4.3 percent. Grades 11 to 12 stood out at 3.4 percent, much lower than nearby education groups. The biggest education shift from 2022 was among post-secondary women, where traditional use rose from 9.4 percent to 13.9 percent, driven by withdrawal rising from 6.6 percent to 12.1 percent.

sections.heading: Wealth differences were small, and the middle group was highest
sections.body: Traditional contraceptive use did not steadily rise or fall with wealth in 2025. The middle wealth quintile had the highest level at 9.2 percent, followed by the highest quintile at 8.4 percent and the second quintile at 8.0 percent. The lowest quintile was at 7.0 percent, and the fourth quintile was at 7.6 percent. Compared with 2022, the largest declines were in the lowest quintile, down from 10.1 percent to 7.0 percent, and the fourth quintile, down from 10.4 percent to 7.6 percent.

takeaways: Traditional contraceptive use among all women fell to 8.1 percent in 2025, and withdrawal made up most of both the level and the drop from 2022.
Regional differences were the clearest divide: Eastern Visayas and Ilocos Region were near 12 percent, while BARMM was much lower at 3.2 percent.
The background patterns were uneven rather than step-by-step, with use highest among women aged 35 to 44, women with one or two children, post-secondary women, and the middle wealth quintile.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
